title: Shorshe Chingri

ingredients.name: large prawns, peeled and deveined
visual_checks: Leave the tails on for presentation. Fresh or thawed frozen prawns both work well.
ingredients.notes: Leave the tails on for presentation. Fresh or thawed frozen prawns both work well.

subs.name: 500 g firm white fish fillets (like rohu or catla), cut into 3 cm cubes — cook for 5–6 minutes total and handle gently to avoid breaking.

ingredients.name: yellow and black mustard seeds (for paste)
visual_checks: Use a 2:1 ratio of yellow to black mustard seeds for a balanced paste that is pungent but not bitter. Soak in water for 15 minutes before grinding.
ingredients.notes: Use a 2:1 ratio of yellow to black mustard seeds for a balanced paste that is pungent but not bitter. Soak in water for 15 minutes before grinding.

subs.name: 3 tbsp ready-made kasundi (Bengali mustard sauce) dissolved in 3 tbsp water — kasundi is available at Bengali stores and captures the authentic flavour.

ingredients.name: green chillies (4 for paste, 2 slit for cooking)
visual_checks: Grind 4 with the mustard paste; slit 2 and add them whole during cooking.
ingredients.notes: Grind 4 with the mustard paste; slit 2 and add them whole during cooking.

ingredients.name: mustard oil
visual_checks: Heat to smoking point and cool slightly before using — this mellows the sharp raw edge of mustard oil and is essential to authentic flavour.
ingredients.notes: Heat to smoking point and cool slightly before using — this mellows the sharp raw edge of mustard oil and is essential to authentic flavour.

subs.name: Cold-pressed coconut oil adds a different but pleasant character; avoid other neutral oils as they flatten the flavour.

ingredients.name: kalonji (nigella seeds)
visual_checks: Used in the tempering; adds a characteristic Bangladeshi/Bengali aroma.
ingredients.notes: Used in the tempering; adds a characteristic Bangladeshi/Bengali aroma.

ingredients.name: water
visual_checks: Just enough to thin the paste into a pourable sauce.
ingredients.notes: Just enough to thin the paste into a pourable sauce.

steps.text: Soak the mustard seeds in 4 tbsp water for 15 minutes to soften them. Transfer to a mixie jar with 4 green chillies, ¼ tsp turmeric, a pinch of salt, and 2 tbsp fresh water. Grind to a very fine, smooth paste — any graininess in the mustard will leave a bitter taste.

Toss the peeled prawns with the remaining ¼ tsp turmeric and a pinch of salt. Set aside for 5 minutes. Heat the mustard oil in a kadai over high heat until it reaches smoking point — wisps of smoke will appear. Remove from heat and allow to cool for 1 minute; this neutralises the sharp raw pungency of mustard oil.

Return the kadai to medium heat. Add the kalonji seeds and let them crackle for 20 seconds. Add the slit green chillies and stir briefly. Pour in the mustard paste and stir for 2 minutes over medium heat until the paste is heated through and begins to dry slightly on the pan.

Add 100 ml water to the mustard paste and stir to form a thin, yellow sauce. Bring to a gentle simmer. Taste the sauce for salt and adjust — it should be boldly pungent and slightly warm from the green chilli.

Add the marinated prawns in a single layer. Cook on medium heat for 3–4 minutes, turning once, until the prawns have turned pink-orange and just curled — do not overcook or they will turn rubbery. The sauce should coat each prawn in a thin, fragrant film. Serve immediately with steamed rice.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Cook seafood until safely done for the species and preparation; keep it chilled before cooking.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: prawn → 500 g firm white fish fillets (like rohu or catla), cut into 3 cm cubes — cook for 5–6 minutes total and handle gently to avoid breaking.; mustard-seeds → 3 tbsp ready-made kasundi (Bengali mustard sauce) dissolved in 3 tbsp water — kasundi is available at Bengali stores and captures the authentic flavour.; mustard-oil → Cold-pressed coconut oil adds a different but pleasant character; avoid other neutral oils as they flatten the flavour.
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Mustard oil is sold across North India and Bengal; heat it to smoking as instructed to mellow its bite. The grinding step works perfectly in a small Indian mixie jar. Yellow mustard seeds may be sold as 'peeli sarson' in North Indian markets; black mustard seeds (kadugu/rai) are in every South Indian pantry. Kasundi (Bengali mustard sauce) is available online and in Bengali grocery stores across Indian metros.
